title: How Much Profit to Keep in the Business at EOFY: The Question Most Owners Skip
linkedin:
June 30 is four days away. The tax conversation is running. The distribution conversation often is not.

How much of this year's profit should stay in the business, and how much should come out?

Most owners answer this based on tax advice. That is the right starting point. But the tax question and the balance sheet question are not the same, and the second one rarely gets equal attention before June 30 closes.

Here is what the balance sheet shows after distributions: when profit leaves the business, retained equity leaves with it. The P&L looks strong. The balance sheet reflects what remains.

For businesses planning 15 to 20 percent revenue growth in FY27, working capital requirements will rise. The cost base grows before the revenue arrives. That additional working capital comes from somewhere, and retained equity is the most efficient source. An overdraft drawn to replace it carries interest.

The compounding pattern is worth understanding: five or six years of sensible annual distributions, each reasonable on its own, can leave a business with $3 million in revenue and $50,000 in net equity.

Lenders assessing FY27 facility applications look at net asset position as well as earnings. Strong EBITDA alongside minimal retained equity often constrains borrowing options more than the profit performance implies.

The question worth answering before June 30: does the business need the equity more than the owner does right now?

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#FinancialStrategy #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
With June 30 arriving this week, it is worth thinking beyond the tax conversation to the balance sheet one.

The question: how much of this year's profit does the business actually need to retain heading into FY27, and how much can comfortably come out as a distribution?

Most years this gets decided based on tax advice alone. The balance sheet implication, what that retained equity means for borrowing capacity, working capital, and financial flexibility in the new year, often comes second.

Has your current equity position kept pace with the demands FY27 will make on the business?

#AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
google:
With the end of the financial year arriving on 30 June, many business owners across Brisbane and the East Coast are making decisions about how much of this year's profit to retain in the business and how much to distribute to themselves as owners.

Most approach this decision primarily from a tax perspective. The balance sheet implication is equally worth considering: how that retained equity will affect borrowing capacity, working capital, and financial strength going into FY27.

A business that distributes most of its annual profit and then needs to fund FY27 growth through its overdraft has effectively moved capital from one place to another, at the cost of the interest to move it back. Retained equity reinvested in working capital carries no equivalent cost.

The period immediately after EOFY is also when banks assess new and increased facility applications. Lenders look at net asset position as well as earnings. A stronger balance sheet on 30 June produces a stronger starting position for any facility conversation in the new financial year.

If you want to understand what your June 30 balance sheet position means for your capital options in FY27, read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
